title: Draughtproofing the weight-box channel in a sash window
subtitle: Sealing the pulley pockets and parting bead without affecting the sash weights
excerpt: The weight-box channels on either side of a box sash window are significant draught paths. The pulley holes allow air to travel from the box cavity directly into the room. Sealing them with purpose-made brush strips and foam plugs takes around an hour per window and reduces air infiltration markedly without affecting window operation.
sourceNotes: Historic England guidance on energy efficiency improvements to traditional windows. Remmers draught-proofing strip technical data. SPAB (Society for the Protection of Ancient Buildings) technical advice on sash windows.

glossaryTerms.definition: The central vertical strip in a box sash window that separates the inner and outer sash grooves. Also called the parting slip.
The innermost vertical beading strip in a box sash window, against which the inner sash slides.

content.text: A Victorian or Edwardian box sash window has four main air-infiltration routes: the meeting rail gap between the upper and lower sash, the staff-bead to sash clearance on both sides, the sill gap at the bottom of the lower sash, and the pulley pockets at the top of each weight-box channel. Standard draughtproofing kits address the first three. The pulley pockets are often overlooked.

The pulley pocket is the small pressed-steel axle plate with a gap around it where the sash cord runs over the pulley wheel. The gap is typically 10 to 15 mm wide and runs the full width of the pulley housing. Behind it, the weight box is an uninsulated cavity that communicates directly with the external air through the base of the box frame. A test with a smoke pencil next to the pulley on a windy day confirms the draught.
Materials
Brush pile strip (9 mm x 4 mm): the same product used on the sash faces. Available on a self-adhesive backing. Cut to length to fill the gap around the pulley housing.
Flexible foam backer rod (10 mm diameter): used to fill the larger gap behind the
parting bead
if the bead has separated from the frame.
Draught-seal putty or flexible filler: for the gap at the sill where the outer sash frame meets the external cill.
Pulley pocket sealing
With the sash raised (outer sash) or lowered (inner sash), expose the pulley housing. Clean the face around the pulley plate with a dry cloth.
Cut brush pile strip to length. Peel the backing and press against the face of the sash-frame recess around three sides of the pulley plate, leaving the bottom gap for cord clearance.
Test: with the sash back in position, check the cord still runs freely over the pulley without binding on the brush strip. The strip should touch the sash face but not resist movement.
Parting-bead gap
The
parting bead

sits in a groove in the pulley stile. Over time it shrinks or warps, leaving a gap. If a gap exceeds 2 mm, gently remove the parting bead (pull straight out; it is friction-fit), clean the groove, and refit with flexible sealant on each face. Do not glue it: the bead needs to be removable for cord replacement.
If the bead is cracked or missing, a replacement parting bead (typically 10 mm x 6 mm softwood) can be cut to length and fitted. Source from a sash-window specialist or millwork supplier.
Staff-bead and meeting-rail brush strips
See the guide on
draught-stripping sash windows
for the
staff-bead
and meeting-rail procedure. That tutorial covers the main sash faces; this one focuses only on the weight-box and pulley paths that are often missed in a standard draughtproofing kit.
Done well vs done badly

Done well: pulley pockets sealed with brush strip, parting beads fitting snugly, sash cords running freely. A smoke-pencil test at the pulley shows no draught. The window still operates smoothly in both directions.

Done badly: brush strip applied too thickly, binds the cord, cord wear accelerated. Or: parting bead glued in place, cord replacement now requires cutting the bead. Either result damages a window that may be original period joinery.
